title: The Silent World
subtitle: Evaluation and salvage treatment for sudden sensorineural hearing loss
description: Definition, prognostic factors, imaging exclusion, steroid therapy, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, and intratympanic salvage treatment for sudden sensorineural hearing loss.

steps.title: Step 1/6: Clinical Presentation and Definition
steps.scenario: A 70-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because she suddenly could not hear from her right ear starting yesterday. She also reports left-sided tinnitus. Brain CT in the emergency department has excluded hemorrhage. Pure-tone audiometry shows severe right hearing loss up to 95 dB and 60 dB in the left ear. Tympanograms are type A bilaterally, indicating normal middle-ear pressure.
steps.media: Load image: Pure-tone audiogram showing profound right sensorineural hearing loss
steps.question: According to the standard clinical definition of sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), which statement is correct?

options.text: (A) More than 20 dB hearing loss at a single frequency within 1 week.
(B) Conductive hearing loss greater than 40 dB with tympanic membrane rupture within 24 hours.
(C) More than 30 dB hearing loss over 3 contiguous audiometric frequencies within 72 hours.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: C

Clinical definition: SSNHL is an otologic emergency. The standard definition is rapid onset within 72 hours with more than 30 dB hearing loss across 3 contiguous audiometric frequencies. Up to 99% of cases are unilateral, and most have no identifiable cause, so they are classified as idiopathic SSNHL.

steps.title: Step 2/6: Prognostic Factors
steps.scenario: While explaining the condition to this 70-year-old patient's family, they anxiously ask, "Doctor, is there a chance her hearing will fully recover?"
steps.media: Load image: Prognostic-factor radar chart including age, vertigo, hearing-loss severity, and treatment timing
steps.question: Based on the literature, which poor prognostic factors does this patient have when estimating the chance of recovery in ISSNHL?

options.text: (A) Female sex and pre-existing hearing loss in the left ear only.
(B) Age over 60, associated vertigo or dizziness, and extremely severe initial hearing loss at 95 dB.
(C) Very short time to presentation only.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: B

Attending tip: Four key factors influence recovery in ISSNHL. Earlier treatment improves prognosis, but age greater than 60 years, associated vertigo or dizziness, severe hearing loss such as 95 dB, and a downward-sloping audiogram suggest poor prognosis. This patient unfortunately has several adverse factors.

steps.title: Step 3/6: Imaging and Differential Diagnosis
steps.scenario: Although brain CT in the emergency department excluded hemorrhage, as an otolaryngologist you must exclude dangerous non-idiopathic retrocochlear causes of unilateral sensorineural hearing loss.
steps.media: Load image: Imaging features of a cerebellopontine angle tumor
steps.question: To exclude acoustic neuroma or multiple sclerosis, which imaging study is the required gold-standard examination?

options.text: (A) Non-contrast high-resolution CT of the temporal bone

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: C

Diagnostic pitfall: About 10-20% of patients with acoustic neuroma or vestibular schwannoma can present with sudden hearing loss. Although the overall proportion is not high, contrast-enhanced MRI is essential to exclude these tumors and central nervous system disease. If MRI is contraindicated, contrast-enhanced CT is the fallback option.

steps.title: Step 4/6: Primary Medical Therapy and Monitoring
steps.scenario: The patient is admitted. You decide to give her a 1-week course of intravenous or oral steroid therapy.
steps.question: What is the most widely accepted initial steroid dose for ISSNHL, and what should be closely monitored in this 70-year-old patient with hypertension?

options.text: (B) Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, usually up to 60 mg/day, for 10-14 days; closely monitor blood glucose and blood pressure.
(C) Dexamethasone 60 mg/day for 1 month; monitor auditory nerve atrophy.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: B

Treatment consensus: The systemic steroid regimen with the best supporting evidence is oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, usually capped at 60 mg/day, for 10-14 days. Because high-dose steroids can cause hypertension, hyperglycemia even in non-diabetic patients, insomnia, and gastric ulceration, admission with close blood pressure and glucose monitoring is strongly recommended in elderly patients.

steps.title: Step 5/6: Adjuvant Therapy: Hyperbaric Oxygen
steps.scenario: Because the patient's hearing loss is very severe, you also arrange 5 sessions of hyperbaric oxygen therapy.
steps.media: Load image: Hyperbaric chamber treatment setting
steps.question: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is thought to increase oxygen delivery to inner-ear tissue and counter cochlear ischemia. Before treatment, which common adverse effect must you warn the patient about?

options.text: (B) Difficulty equalizing middle-ear pressure, causing ear fullness, pain, or Eustachian tube dysfunction

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: B

Counseling point: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is time-consuming and expensive. Serious complications are uncommon, but 17-45% of patients experience middle-ear pressure equalization difficulty or Eustachian tube dysfunction. Claustrophobia is also a common reason for treatment interruption.

steps.title: Step 6/6: Salvage Therapy: Intratympanic Steroid
steps.scenario: After systemic steroids and 5 sessions of hyperbaric oxygen therapy, the patient has only a poor response and her right-sided hearing remains very poor. You suggest intratympanic steroid injection to the family.
steps.media: Load image: Microscopic transtympanic steroid injection through the tympanic membrane
steps.question: For intratympanic steroid as salvage therapy, what are its major advantage and main complication?

options.text: (A) Advantage: avoids systemic steroid adverse effects such as hyperglycemia and achieves higher local inner-ear concentration; complication: persistent tympanic membrane perforation.
(B) Advantage: one injection guarantees 100% hearing recovery; complication: irreversible facial paralysis.
(C) Advantage: completely covered by insurance and painless; complication: middle-ear cholesteatoma.

steps.feedback:
Correct answer: A

Procedure interpretation: When systemic steroids fail or when patients have severe diabetes or hypertension that makes systemic therapy unsuitable, intratympanic steroid injection is an excellent salvage option. It increases inner-ear drug concentration and avoids systemic adverse effects. Its main complications are persistent tympanic membrane perforation after repeated injections, along with temporary dizziness and pain.
